topic: A student finds out their scholarship was meant for someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp parchment felt like sandpaper beneath my fingertips as I unfolded the official notification. My stomach tightened into a knot, a cold, heavy pressure that radiated outward. The words confirmed what I already suspected: the prestigious merit scholarship, the one I’d poured countless sleepless nights into securing, was designated for someone else. A slow, bitter smile touched my lips, thin and utterly devoid of warmth. I folded the paper with deliberate, slow movements, the sound echoing too loudly in the otherwise silent library. I looked up, meeting the gaze of a passing student, and offered only a brief, dismissive flick of my eyes before turning back to the towering shelves of forgotten knowledge. It was an insult, a blatant misdirection, and I felt a profound sense of superiority wash over me as I tucked the evidence away.

[story 2]

"Well, isn't this rich," I muttered, the sound barely audible above the hum of the ventilation system. I stared at the typed confirmation, my jaw clenching so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. The realization hit me like a physical blow—all that effort, all that sacrifice, had been aimed at an empty vessel. A sharp, unpleasant sensation prickled behind my eyes. I let out a short, humorless laugh, a sound that scraped against the quiet atmosphere. I didn't bother to reread the fine print; the intent was crystal clear. I carefully slid the document into my binder, the leather cover feeling suddenly heavy with undeserved importance. I stood up, smoothing down my jacket with an exaggerated air of nonchalance, already planning how to make the next few weeks entirely about ignoring this embarrassing oversight.

[story 3]

I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning in protest under my weight. The letter lay spread across my desk, mocking me with its official seal. A slow, almost imperceptible tightening around my mouth was the only outward sign of the turmoil churning inside. It wasn't anger, not exactly; it was something colder, something that assessed the situation with detached, clinical judgment. I picked up a pen, turning it over and over until the plastic felt slick. The recipient of the scholarship, some distant, unknown figure, was going to benefit from my miscalculation. A wave of profound self-satisfaction washed over me, a feeling of being smarter, more perceptive than the system itself. I capped the pen with a decisive click, the sound sharp and final. The matter was settled; it was simply a footnote in my superior academic trajectory.
